coredata.dc:title: Copper doped carbon dots as the multi-functional fluorescent sensing platform for tetracyclines and pH
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Jianhua
Lu, Wenjing
Zhang, Huilin
Meng, Yating
Du, Fangfang
Shuang, Shaomin
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: The copper doped fluorescent carbon dots (Cu-CDs) with excellent biocompatibility were prepared from l-tryptophan and copper chloride as precursors through a facilely hydrothermal method. As-prepared Cu-CDs exhibited the bright blue fluorescence and excellent optical stability. In the presence of tetracyclines antibiotics (TCs), the intensely fluorescent quenching of Cu-CDs occurred from internal filtration effect (IFE), providing the foundation of TCs quantification. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs decreased gradually along with the increasing of pH and a sigmoidal curve was well fitted with the pKa12.49 and pKa2 6.21, respectively. The Cu-CDs as a multi-functional fluorescence sensing platform demonstrated great accuracy and selectivity for tetracyclines (TCs) and pH. On account of practical application, as-synthesized Cu-CDs not only were effectively employed for determination of TCs in antibiotic tablets and milk products, but also could trace nuclear and had been extended to sense and visualize pH fluctuations in cells due to low toxicity and biocompatibility. As a facile and effective multi-functional fluorescent sensor, the proposed Cu-CDs have a significant potential for biosensing and biomedical imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu-CDs
Tetracyclines
pH
Multi-functional
Sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of antibiotics by the Cu-CDs
Detection of TCs in real sample

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Principle demonstration of the synthesized route of the Cu-CDs and the Cu-CDs as fluorescent sensing platform to detect tetracyclines antibiotics and pH.

(a) TEM image of the Cu-CDs, inset: corresponding HRTEM images and the size distribution of Cu-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectra of Cu-CDs. (c) XPS spectra and (d-e) The high-resolution N1s, Cu 2p spectrum of Cu-CDs. (f) The UV absorption spectrum of Cu-CDs and FL spectra of Cu-CDs.

(a) Relative fluorescence decrement of the Cu-CDs in the presence of various inspected substances. Several antibiotics and other interfering species: 100 μM. (b) Fluorescence spectra of the Cu-CDs adding to different concentrations of OTC. (c-e): Relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of OTC, TC, CTC. (f) UV absorption OTC (black), and excitation (red) and emission (blue) fluorescence spectra of Cu-CDs.
Zeta potential measurements of Cu-CDs, Cu-CDs + OTC and CDs.

(a) The FL spectra of Cu-CDs (0.7 mg/mL) with pH ranging from 1.0 to 8.0 at λex of 369 nm. (b) Fitting curves between relative fluorescence decrease and pH. (c) The linear relationships between fluorescence intensity and pH (1.8 to 2.8). (d) The linear relationships between fluorescence intensity and pH (5.6 to 6.8), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the Cu-CDs at pH 2.0 and other pH, correspondingly.

(a) and (d) LSCM images of acridine orange, the Cu-CDs (0.7 mg/mL) in HeLa cells. (b) and (e) bright-field images. (c) and (f) overlay images of (a) + (b) and (d) + (e). (g), (h) the images of Acridine orange and Cu-CDs co-cultured with HeLa cells for 40 min, (i) overlay image of (g) and (h).
LSCM images of the Cu-CDs (0.7 mg/mL) in HeLa cells at pH (a) 2.0, (d) 4.0 and (g) 6.0. (b), (e) and (h) are bright-field images. (c), (f) and (i) are overlay images.
Determination of three TCs in real samples with standard addition method.

head.ce:title: Copper doped carbon dots as the multi-functional fluorescent sensing platform for tetracyclines and pH

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering and Institute of Environmental Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The copper doped fluorescent carbon dots (Cu-CDs) with excellent biocompatibility were prepared from l-tryptophan and copper chloride as precursors through a facilely hydrothermal method. As-prepared Cu-CDs exhibited the bright blue fluorescence and excellent optical stability. In the presence of tetracyclines antibiotics (TCs), the intensely fluorescent quenching of Cu-CDs occurred from internal filtration effect (IFE), providing the foundation of TCs quantification. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs decreased gradually along with the increasing of pH and a sigmoidal curve was well fitted with the pKa12.49 and pKa2 6.21, respectively. The Cu-CDs as a multi-functional fluorescence sensing platform demonstrated great accuracy and selectivity for tetracyclines (TCs) and pH. On account of practical application, as-synthesized Cu-CDs not only were effectively employed for determination of TCs in antibiotic tablets and milk products, but also could trace nuclear and had been extended to sense and visualize pH fluctuations in cells due to low toxicity and biocompatibility. As a facile and effective multi-functional fluorescent sensor, the proposed Cu-CDs have a significant potential for biosensing and biomedical imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted widespread attention owing to numerously superior features, including low toxicity and excellent luminescence, high photostability and facile surface modification [1], consequently demonstrating various potential applications in optical sensing [2,3], biomedicine [4,5], photocatalysis [6,7], and optoelectronic devices [8] and so on. Carbon dots as the fluorescent sensing platforms are amazing toward various analytical targets. Currently, a variety of single-mode sensing systems with carbon dots were constructed for individual analyte, for instance ions [9], amino acids [10] and pH [11] etc. To further improve efficiency of analytical detection, some mutli-functional CDs have been developed for the response to two or more targets. On the basis of different sensing mechanisms, including inner filter effect (IFE), oxidative quenching, photo-induced electron transfer (PET) and so on, have been employed for detecting multiple analytes, such as Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid [12], hypochlorite and peroxynitrite [13], Hg2+, lemon yellow and H2O2 [14], methotrexate, rutin, quercetin and temperature [15] etc. Above researches enormously improved the efficiency of carbon dots as fluoresce sensors, which motivated us to achieve multi-mode sensing by integrating CDs into a functional platform. It has been demonstrated that CDs can achieved multifunctionality through surface modification and heteroatom doping [16,17]. Especially, heteroatom-doping is a more powerful approach owing to nature of facile manipulating, tunability of fluorescence emission and improvement of active sites [18,19]. To date, various nonmetallic heteroatoms, including N [10], S [20], B [21] and Si [22] doped CDs were capable of metal ions detection, temperature sensing, and cellular imaging, which have broadened the applications in the area of multi-targets detection and bioimaging.

However, few studies were reported metal ion doped carbon dots so far. Compared to nonmetallic heteroatoms, metal ions particularly the transition metal ions have more electrons and unoccupied orbitals, which make it easier to change electronic structures associated with the energy gap of CDs, offer more active sites and impart novel functionalities to the doped CDs [23]. Sun et al. firstly introduced Zn2+ doped carbon dots achieving much higher photoluminescence quantum yields, which represented a promising platform for highly photoluminescent dots [24]. Yuan et al. further constructed Fe-CDs for detection of dopamine and visualization of dynamic invasions of dopamine into HeLa cells, which may be ascribed to dopamine encompassing catechol groups formed multiplexes with Fe3+ due to coordination, following oxidation to produce hydroquinone and quenching of the fluorescence of CDs [25]. Mn [26], La [27] and Gd [28] doped carbon dots were sequentially emerged for volatile organic amines sensing, multicolor imaging of cells and tissue, bacteriostasis, gene delivery, and MRI probe. Unfortunately, some metals are harmful to living cells and organism and thus it is necessary to develop metal-doped CDs with low cytotoxicity. Copper as an important trace element for biological system, has been reported that have ignorable toxic character [29]. To date, Cu-CDs are mainly applied to photocatalytic materials for the catalytic reaction of p-nitropheno [30], p-phenylenediamine (PPD) [31], and glucose [32] etc. Recently, Cu-CDs as fluorescence probes have also attracted attention. Several Cu-CDs single sensing systems were constructed for hydrogen sulfide [33], pyrogallol [34] and isoniazid [35]. The Cu-CDs with multi-function sensing has been exploited [36,37]. It remains a challenging matter to develop Cu-CDs toward multi-targets detection.

Tetracyclines (TCs) have been broadly utilized in animal husbandry additive, sterilization, and drug. However, the abuse of TCs carries tremendous impairment to human, and people will continue to passively absorb residues through the food chain, leading to tolerance, hearing injury and liver damage [38–40]. Hence, it is of great significance to develop of TCs analysis toward CDs multi-mode sensing. In addition, intracellular pH is one of the most essential factor which is related to numerous life trials, including intracellular proliferation, apoptosis, enzymatic activity and so on [41]. Moreover, a slight variation in pH is linked closely to cell dysfunction and causing many diseases, for instance, rheumatoid inflammation, astonishment, and malignance [42]. Based on the above concerns, we attempt to construct a multi-mode based on Cu-CDs sensing platform for monitoring of TCs and pH.

Herein, we proposed a one-pot solvothermal routine to synthesis copper, doped CDs (Cu-CDs) by using l-tryptophan and copper chloride as precursors. In the presence of TCs, the fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs was dramatically quenched owing to internal filtration effect (IFE). And this built quantitative basis of TCs. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the Cu-CDs presented obvious decrease with the pH increasing. The pKa values of the Cu-CDs were proved to be 2.49 and 6.21, respectively. The Cu-CDs as a multi-functional fluorescence sensing platform demonstrated great accuracy and selectivity for tetracyclines (TCs) and pH. Moreover, Cu-CDs were not only used for detection of TCs in antibiotic tablets and milk products, but also could trace nuclear and monitor pH fluctuation in living cells owing to low toxicity and biocompatibility. The multi-functional fluorescent sensor based on Cu-CDs has a fantastically prospect for biosensing and biomedical imaging (Scheme 1 ).

First, 0.10 g l-Tryptophan and 0.17 g CuCl2·2H2O were dissolved in 20 mL of water. After that, the clear blue solution was obtained through ultrasonic vibration for 15 min, the solution was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and reacted at 180 °C for 3 h. Subsequently, the Cu-CDs crude product was purified by filtration. Afterward, the suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. Then, the solution was further purified with a MD34 dialysis bag (100 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the orange products were acquired by freeze-drying. The CDs without Cu doping were also synthesized with 0.10 g l-Tryptophan as the only precursor by the identical method for a contrast experiment.

Oxytetracycline (OTC) was chose as a typical testing model for TCs. The Cu-CDs were diluted with Brittone Robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH = 4) and gave a last concentration of 0.7 mg/mL. After, the fluorescence emission spectra of the samples were recorded by introducing different concentrations of OTC. The contrast experiments using CDs without Cu doping were also conducted for determination of antibiotics. The selectivity of the probe for TCs was measured by introducing various other kinds of antibiotics, some ions, amino acids, small molecules replacing TCs with the same experimental conditions. The fluorescence emissions were determined between 380 nm and 600 nm at an excitation of the 369 nm.

The pH was determined at different pH in (BR) buffer. 104 μL of the Cu-CDs (0.7 mg mL−1) solution was dispersed into the 2.5 mL of BR at various pH. After equilibration of 3 min, the fluorescent emission spectra of the solutions were attained.

The method utilized to determine TC and OTC tablets were bought from local Baiou drugstore. Sample processing was as follow: the antibiotic tablet was weighed precisely and ground to powder. A certain amount of pharmaceutical powder was weighed into a 10 mL centrifuge tube and then the drug powder was dissolved under ultrasonic condition after HCl (10 mL, 1 M) was introduced [43]. To end, the solution was analyzed by the Cu-CDs.

Milk samples were bought from a local supermarket. Firstly, milk was pretreated according to a conventional step referring to earlier reports [44,45]. In brief, 20 mL of acetonitrile was added into 10 mL milk. Afterward, the suspensions of mixture were sonicated for 15 min and centrifuged for 10 min at 8000 rpm. Then, the collected supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm pore diameter micro porous membrane. Lastly, the filtrate was 10-fold diluted with (BR) buffer (pH = 4). The three TCs (20, 30 and 40 μM) were introduced into the aforementioned samples. The method of measurement was identical to the above mentioned procedure.

HeLa cells in DMEM medium were incubated in each dish at 37 °C for 48 h. Then, the treated cells were washed three times with PBS (pH = 7.4), after the medium was removed. The Cu-CDs (0.7 mg mL−1) were added to HeLa cell culture medium to incubate for 40 min. As a control group, the cells were stained with 2 μL diluted acridine orange dye solution for 40 min. Last, wash the fluorescent dye with PBS for three times, which did not enter the cell. Imaging of pH in cells: the Cu-CDs solution was cultured with the cells for another 30 min using different pH BR buffers. Afterwards, the cells were washed three times again with PBS (pH = 7.4). The cellular imaging was implemented using confocal laser scanning microscopy.

The prepared Cu-CDs had a uniform spherical morphology, which was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). The Cu-CDs had an average size of 4.84 nm and the size mostly distributed between 2.05 nm and 5.55 nm in Fig. 1 a. Simultaneously, the HRTEM image exhibited that the Cu-CDs presented obvious lattice spacing of 0.31 nm, which was in accordance with the plane (100) of graphite carbon [46] (Fig. 1a). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. 1b) showed the broad peak from 3000−3500 cm−1 and 2900−2940 cm−1 belonged to OH/NH and C H stretching vibrations, respectively [47]. The distinguishing peaks of 1398 cm−1 and 1186 cm−1 corresponded to the CN/ CO stretching, the absorption peak of 1736 cm−1 was attributed to the CO of the alicyclic group or the alicyclic amine group. Moreover, the strong peaks at 1582 and 1090 cm−1 finely matched with stretching vibration of CC and CuN [48]. Also, inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES) have concluded the results, which copper ions accounted for 10.6 % in Cu-CDs. Moreover, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the Cu-CDs exhibited four characteristic peaks at 288 eV, 401 eV, 533 eV and 934 eV (Fig. 1 c), corresponding to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Cu2p, respectively [32,49–51]. The high-resolution C1s spectrum showed three main peaks of CC/CC, CN/CO and CO, at 284.5 eV, 285.0 eV, 288.7 eV, individually (Fig. S1a). N1s was divided into double peaks of 399.9 eV and 401.7 eV, indicative of the existence of pyridine N and pyrrole N (Fig. 1d), as displayed in the N1s high-resolution spectrum. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum, the peaks at 531.8 eV and 532.8 were linked to COH and CO, individually (Fig. S1b). The Cu 2p XPS spectrum revealed two peaks locating at 934.5 and 954.6 eV, which could be ascribed to the spin-orbit splitting of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 1e).

The optical properties of as-prepared Cu-CDs were investigated by UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra and quantum yield (QY). As depicted in Fig. 1f, the UV–vis spectra demonstrated obvious absorption peaks at 278.0 nm, which may be ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic CC bond. The Cu-CDs exhibited the excitation independent emission (Fig. S2a) with the maximum fluorescent emission 464 nm at excitation 369 nm. By choosing quinine sulfate in water, the QY of the Cu-CDs was calculated to be 9.5 %, while the QY of CDs without doping was only 2.1 %. The effects of ionic intensity and radiation time on the optical stability of Cu-CDs were studied. Different ionic strength environments were simulated with different concentrations of NaCl. Even when the concentration of NaCl solution reached 2.0 mol L−1, the fluorescence (FL) intensity could still reached 83.8 % of the original intensity (Fig. S2b). The fluorescence intensity of Cu-CDs was almost constant under 90 min of irradiation by xenon arc light (Fig. S2c). Above results prove that the Cu-CDs own outstanding fluorescence stability. Above properties demonstrate that Cu-CDs as a fluorescence sensor have great prospect in complex systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of antibiotics by the Cu-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As numerous active functional groups exist on the surface of the Cu-CDs, we explored the prospective application of the sensors. Interestingly, Cu-CDs could directly determine tetracyclines (TCs). The addition of TCs carried considerably fluorescence quenching of Cu-CDs. To verify the selectivity of the Cu-CDs to TCs, the effect of other kinds of antibiotics (AMP, STR, ERY, NOR, CHL), some common metal ions, amino acids, and biologically related molecules (Glu, AA, BSA) were investigated. As displayed in Fig. 2 a, only three kinds of TCs (OTC, CTC and TC) could obviously quench the fluorescence of the Cu-CDs, and several interfering substances had little effect. The results exhibit that the Cu-CDs have good specificity for the determination of tetracyclines antibiotics and are expected to be an efficient fluorescent sensor for TCs. In this study, we choose OTC as model molecules for detection TCs. As displayed in Fig. 2b, the FL intensity of Cu-CDs gradually decreased with the concentration increasing of OTC (0−116 μM). In Fig. 2c, the quantity of OTC ranging from 2−44 μM, the quenching efficiency of the Cu-CDs presented an excellent linear relationship and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 0.16 μM. Similarly, the linear ranges of TC and CTC were 2–32 μM and 4–56 μM, respectively (Fig. 2d and e). The LODs was 0.17 μM for TC and 0.20 μM for CTC, in keeping with the standard 3σ (signal to-noise) criteria (Table S1) [52]. These LODs are comparable sensitivity or superior to the earlier reports (Table S2) [53–56] and fulfill the food safety criterion of European Union for maximum residual limits [57]. As depicted in Fig. S4, compare with Cu-CDs, the sensitivity of all three kinds of TCs detected with CDs as probe was relatively poor. Therefore, we use Cu-CDs as the fluorescence sensor to detect the TCs, which has great selectivity and high sensitivity, and has prospective application in practical sample analysis.

To explore the mechanism of TCs causing fluorescence quenching of Cu-CDs, the UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra, fluorescence lifetimes, zeta potentials and FT-IR spectra were measured were consequently investigated. By using OTC as a model compound, OTC shows an apparent absorption band (200−400 nm), which has a large overlap with the excitation spectrum of the Cu-CDs as displayed in Fig. 2f. It is assumed that the process of the fluorescence quenching by the TCs may be due to an internal filtering effect (IFE) between Cu-CDs and TCs. What's more, the fluorescence lifetimes of the Cu-CDs and Cu-CDs/OTC are determined to be 16.92 and 16.96 ns, individually (Fig. S3), which obviously confirm that no energy transfer process occurs between Cu-CDs and OTC. Above results have proved that the fluorescence quenching of Cu-CDs can be attributed to the IFE based steady quenching mechanism [58]. Besides, the zeta potentials of without doping CDs and Cu-CDs are −17.7 mV and +5.62 mV (Fig. 3 ), respectively, which may be due to the doping of the positively charged copper ions in the CDs. Reversely, by using OTC as a model compound, the zeta potential of Cu-CDs changes to −1.19 mV after introducing OTC (Fig. 3), which can may be ascribed that the chelation effect of Cu and hydroxyls or amino of OTC consumed the positive charge on the surface of the Cu-CDs [59]. Moreover, the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S5a) illustrate that the distinguishing peaks of 1458 cm−1 and 1217 cm−1 appear after the addition of OTC, corresponding to the CH3/CO stretching of OTC. Above results demonstrate that due to the doping of the Cu element on the carbon dots, it is possible to have a strong attraction to the TCs, to form the Cu-CDs-TCs complex, and to change the surface state of the surface of the carbon dots, resulting in a fluorescence quenching.

Since pH plays a vital role in various biological and chemical systems, the monitor of pH is of great significance. We found that the FL intensity of the Cu-CDs considerably decreased at the acid physiological pH, the Cu-CDs could be possibly applied as pH sensor. The FL intensity of the Cu-CDs was changed ranging from pH 1.0–8.0 at excitation 369 nm (Fig. 4 a). The Cu-CDs showed a significant decrease with the increasing of pH. By using BiDoseResp equation, a sigmoidal curve was well fitted:

y=65.53+697.59×
0.44
1+
10
2.50 - x
×
- 0.57
+
0.56
1+
10
6.21-x
×
-1.15
, R2 = 0.995. The FL intensity of Cu-CDs and pH displayed a good linear relationship ranging from 1.8–2.8 and 5.6–6.8 (Fig. 4b). The linear regression equations were obtained, (F0-F)/F0 = 0.2089pH-0.37742, R2 = 0.996 (Fig. 4c); (F0-F)/F0 = 0.46653pH-2.41089, R2 = 0.995 (Fig. 4d). Fig. S5b showed the reversibility of the Cu-CDs to pH. The concentrated HCl and NaOH was utilized to transfer pH of the solution between 2.0 and 6.0 [60]. As depicted in Fig. S5b, it showed that the processes were absolutely reversible. It means that the prepared sensor demonstrates a greatly reversible response to pH. The results shown that the Cu-CDs hold excellent photostability at different pH (2.0, 4.0, 6.0) (Fig. S7a). Therefore, the Cu-CDs can be used for rapid monitoring the pH variability in living cells.

Since the Cu-CDs show a significant response to pH with two pKa, we have explored the mechanism. On the basis of the N1s high-resolution spectrum of the Cu-CDs, lots of nitrogen atoms combing to the edge of the carbon core, mainly exist in the form of pyridine and pyrrole. We speculate Cu-CDs present double pKa during pH determination, which may be attributed to the special structure. Since a lone pair of electrons of the nitrogen atoms on pyrrole participate in p-π conjugation, the basicity of pyridinic is comparatively stronger than pyrrolic. When the pH decrease, the nitrogen atoms on pyridine are firstly protonated and displayed the linearity ranging from 5.6–6.8. As the concentration of H+ continues to increase, the nitrogen atoms on pyrrole are subsequently protonated and presented the linearity range of pH 1.8–2.8 [61]. The quenching of fluorescence of the Cu-CDs are mainly ascribed to protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups, indicative of by those zeta potential varies from 32.3 to −8.58 mV (Fig. S6) with the increasing of pH (2.0–6.0) [62]. This observation of pH-switched fluorescence reveals that the Cu-CDs can be applid as a prospective pH sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TCs in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: For exploring the suitability of the proposed sensor in facing up actual complex samples, the Cu-CDs were made for the detection of TCs in antibiotic tablets and milk samples. The oxytetracycline tablets and tetracycline tablets were acquired from a drugstore and the milk was bought from the supermarket. The recoveries of OTC, and TC in antibiotic tablets were calculated to be 96.8–100.7 %, with RSDs of 3.5–5.4 %. Moreover, when individually adding three different concentrations of TCs in milk (20, 30 and 40 μM), the average recoveries were accomplished, for instance 103.5–107.5 % for OTC, 94.5–110.9 % for TC, and 102.7–117.8 for CTC, and RSDs were lower than 5.8 % (Table 1 ). Therefore, above results demonstrate feasibility of this sensor.

In conclusion, a facile and effective method for synthesis of Cu doped CDs by using l-tryptophan and copper chloride as precursors was developed. In the presence of TCs, they were capable of quenching the fluorescence of Cu-CDs owing to internal filtration effect (IFE), which built the foundation of TCs quantification. In addition, the Cu-CDs revealed a remarkable response to pH and showed good pH-dependent fluorescence with double pKa (2.49, 6.21). The Cu-CDs as a multi-functional fluorescence sensing platform was able to response to both tetracyclines (TCs) and pH. Moreover, Cu-CDs not only were used for detection of TCs in antibiotic tablets and milk products, but also could trace nuclear and monitor pH changes in living cells. The multi-functional fluorescent sensor based on Cu-CDs has a fantastically prospect for biosensing and biomedical imaging.
Jianhua Guo: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing -original draft,Writing- review & editing.
Wenjing Lu: Project administration, Supervision, Discussed the results.
Huilin Zhang: Formal analysis, Discussed the results.
Yating Meng: Investigation, Discussed the results.
Fangfang Du: Supervision.
Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision.
Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21874087), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province under Grant (No. 201901D211155) Postgraduate student education innovation project (NO. 2020BY005) and Research Project Supported by Shanxi Scholarship Council of China (No. 2020-003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.129360.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultrafast responsive and sensitive ratiometric fluorescent pH nanoprobe based on label-free dual-emission carbon dots
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Jianhua Guo She is currently pursuing her Master degree in pharmaceutical analysis at Shanxi University. She research interests mainly focus on the nanomaterials-based application of fluorescent sensors in pharmaceutical analysis.

Wenjing Lu is a lecturer of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her PhD degree from Shanxi University in 2019. Her research interests mainly focus on the construction and analytical application of nanocomposites.

Huilin Zhang received her master degree from Shanxi University in 2020 and now is pursuing her PhD degree in Environmental Science at Shanxi University. Her research interests mainly focus on the nanomaterials-based application of fluorescent sensors in analytical chemistry.

Yating Meng received her master degree from Shanxi University in 2020 and now is pursuing her PhD degree in Environmental Science at Shanxi University. Her research interests mainly focus on the nanomaterials-based application of fluorescent sensors.

Fangfang Du received her master degree from Lvliang University in 2017 and now is pursuing her PhD degree in Environmental Science at Shanxi University. Her research interests mainly focus on the applications of fluorescent probe for biosenors.

Shaomin Shuang is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. She received her Ph.D. degree in South China University of Technology in 1998. Her research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry, supermolecular chemistry and photochemsity.

Chuan Dong is a professor of Analytical Chemistry at Shanxi University. He received his Ph.D. degree in Shanxi University in 2001. His research interests focus on the areas of analytical chemistry, dyes and pigments and photochemsity.
